title: Quiz: Hardware-Efficient Ansätze
description: Test your understanding of hardware-efficient circuit design

questions.question: Why are hardware-efficient ansätze important for NISQ devices?

options.text: They minimize depth and work within hardware constraints

questions.explanation: Hardware-efficient ansätze are designed to minimize circuit depth and use native gates, working within the limitations of noisy near-term quantum devices.

questions.question: What happens when you use non-native gates in your circuit?

options.text: Nothing, all gates work the same
They must be decomposed into native gates, increasing depth

questions.explanation: Non-native gates must be decomposed into sequences of native gates during compilation, which increases circuit depth and introduces more errors.

questions.question: Which type of gate typically has the highest error rate?
questions.explanation: Two-qubit gates typically have error rates 10-100x higher than single-qubit gates (0.5-2% vs 0.01-0.1%), making them the primary source of circuit errors.

questions.question: Linear (nearest-neighbor) connectivity is always worse than all-to-all connectivity for variational circuits.
questions.explanation: False. While all-to-all seems more powerful, implementing it on hardware with limited connectivity requires many SWAP gates, which add depth and errors. Linear connectivity is often better for NISQ.

questions.question: How can you parallelize entanglement layers in a linear connectivity ansatz?

options.text: Apply all CZ gates at once
Apply even-indexed pairs, then odd-indexed pairs

questions.explanation: By grouping non-overlapping qubit pairs (even: 0-1, 2-3, 4-5; odd: 1-2, 3-4), you can apply each group in parallel, reducing depth from O(n) to O(1) per group.

questions.question: What is a typical coherence time limit for superconducting qubits?
questions.explanation: Superconducting qubits typically have T1/T2 coherence times around 50-200 microseconds, limiting the total circuit execution time before quantum information is lost.

questions.question: If each two-qubit gate has 1% error, how many can you apply before fidelity drops below 50%?
questions.explanation: With 1% error per gate, fidelity ≈ (0.99)^n < 0.5 when n > ln(0.5)/ln(0.99) ≈ 69. So approximately 70 two-qubit gates before fidelity drops below 50%.

questions.question: Which are valid strategies for reducing circuit depth? (Select all)
questions.explanation: Parallelization, native gates, and pruning all reduce depth. Adding more parameters typically increases depth by requiring more gates.
